commonMistakesSection.mistake: Testing only the happy path with valid data
commonMistakesSection.whyWrong: Production data has missing master records, blank values, and timing edge cases; the program dumps the first time it meets them.
commonMistakesSection.correctApproach: Always test positive, negative, and no-data scenarios; add a boundary case for date and amount limits and document each in the TS.

commonMistakesSection.mistake: Skipping ATC because it is 'just warnings'
commonMistakesSection.whyWrong: ATC findings often hide real bugs (SELECT *, missing SY-SUBRC, obsolete tables) that surface as production incidents after go-live.
commonMistakesSection.correctApproach: Treat ATC findings as build-breakers — remediate or document an explicit exemption before releasing the transport.

commonMistakesSection.mistake: Testing only in development and not in QA before production
commonMistakesSection.whyWrong: Dev systems often have toy data and missing customising; QA reveals authorization, performance, and integration issues dev cannot.
commonMistakesSection.correctApproach: Move the transport to QA, repeat the test scenarios with realistic data, and get functional UAT sign-off before production import.

commonMistakesSection.mistake: Not writing ABAP Unit tests for class methods
commonMistakesSection.whyWrong: Regression bugs reappear at every change because there is no automated safety net, and refactoring becomes risky.
commonMistakesSection.correctApproach: Add ABAP Unit test classes covering each public method; run via SE24 -> Class -> Unit Test or integrate into the CI pipeline.

basic-abap-014.quickRevisionNotes:
What: ABAP testing is a structured set of checks from syntax to UAT that protect production from defects.

Used for:
- Catching compile and quality issues early
- Validating positive, negative, and edge-case behavior
- Confirming integration and authorization in QA
- Securing business sign-off via UAT

Key syntax / tables / Tcodes:
- Ctrl+F2 syntax check, ATC / SCI for quality
- ABAP Unit (SE24 -> Class -> Test) for method-level tests
- SE38, SE80, SE37, SAT, ST05 for runtime checks
- STMS for staged transport across DEV / QA / PRD

Interview keyword: Syntax + ATC + Unit + Integration + UAT before production.

followupAnswerBank.question: What is the difference between syntax check and ATC check?
followupAnswerBank.hinglishExplanation: Ctrl+F2 ke through SE38 mein syntax check verify karta hai ki ABAP program errors ke bina compile hota hai — missing variables, wrong syntax, undefined types. Yeh business logic, performance, ya coding standards check nahi karta. ABAP Test Cockpit deep static code analysis perform karta hai: yeh performance anti-patterns jaise SELECT star aur SELECT inside LOOP, missing error handling, deprecated statements, S/4HANA compatibility violations jaise simplified table structures ko directly access karna, aur clean core violations check karta hai. ATC significantly more comprehensive hai aur har transport se pehle mandatory hona chahiye.
followupAnswerBank.interviewAnswer: Syntax check in SE38 via Ctrl+F2 verifies that the ABAP program compiles without errors — missing variables, wrong syntax, undefined types. It does not check business logic, performance, or coding standards. ABAP Test Cockpit performs deep static code analysis: it checks for performance anti-patterns like SELECT star and SELECT inside LOOP, missing error handling, deprecated statements, S/4HANA compatibility violations like accessing simplified table structures directly, and clean core violations. ATC is significantly more comprehensive and should be mandatory before every transport.
followupAnswerBank.realtimeExplanation: Scenario: Program passed syntax check perfectly — compiled with zero errors. ATC ran and found: SELECT star on BSEG (performance critical), missing sy-subrc check after READ TABLE, and direct BSEG access flagged for S/4HANA compatibility. Three real issues that syntax check completely missed. ATC caught what compiler could not.
followupAnswerBank.mistakes: Galti: 'Syntax check pass ho gaya, transport kar do.' Syntax check ne sirf prove kiya ki code compile hota hai — it says nothing about correctness, performance, or quality.
followupAnswerBank.codeExample:
" Syntax check passes — ATC still flags issues
SELECT * FROM bseg INTO TABLE lt_bseg  " ATC: SELECT *!
  WHERE bukrs = '1000'.
READ TABLE lt_bseg INTO ls_line  " ATC: sy-subrc missing!
  WITH KEY hkont = '400000'.
lv_amount = ls_line-dmbtr.  " Wrong if not found!

followupAnswerBank.question: What test scenarios should you always cover in unit testing?
followupAnswerBank.hinglishExplanation: Har ABAP unit test ko minimum teen scenario types cover karne chahiye. Positive test: valid input data exist hai, program correct expected output produce karta hai — yeh happy path verify karta hai. Negative test: invalid ya missing data, program ise gracefully handle karta hai bina dump ke — yeh error handling verify karta hai. Boundary test: edge cases jaise empty internal tables, single row result, maximum data volume, data mein special characters, zero amounts, year-end dates. Additionally, authorization checks wale programs ke liye, actual user role ke saath test karo. Comprehensive unit testing matlab program tested claim karne se pehle saare teen scenario types cover karna.
followupAnswerBank.interviewAnswer: Every ABAP unit test must cover at minimum three scenario types. Positive test: valid input data exists, program produces correct expected output — this verifies the happy path. Negative test: invalid or missing data, program handles it gracefully without dump — this verifies error handling. Boundary test: edge cases like empty internal tables, single row result, maximum data volume, special characters in data, zero amounts, year-end dates. Additionally, for programs with authorization checks, test with the actual user role. Comprehensive unit testing means covering all three scenario types before claiming the program is tested.
followupAnswerBank.realtimeExplanation: Bug in production: month-end report crashed on December 31 every year. Developer tested with normal dates during unit test — passed. Did not test boundary date December 31 where fiscal year calculation behaved differently. One boundary test would have caught this annual production issue.
followupAnswerBank.mistakes: Galti: Sirf positive case test karna — 'data hai, output aaya, done.' Production has all kinds of data — new materials, deleted vendors, missing customizing. Test what can go wrong, not just what should work.
followupAnswerBank.codeExample:
" Three scenarios always test karo
" 1. Positive: valid vendor
SELECT SINGLE name1 FROM lfa1 INTO @DATA(ls_vendor)
  WHERE lifnr = '100001'.  " exists
ASSERT sy-subrc = 0.  " must find
" 2. Negative: invalid vendor
SELECT SINGLE name1 FROM lfa1 INTO @DATA(ls_bad)
  WHERE lifnr = 'INVALID'. " not exists
ASSERT sy-subrc <> 0.  " must NOT find

followupAnswerBank.question: Why is volume testing critical and how do you perform it?
followupAnswerBank.hinglishExplanation: Volume testing verify karta hai ki ABAP code production-scale data ke saath acceptably perform karta hai, sirf development test data ke saath nahi. Approach: basis team ke saath kaam karo production data ka representative sample QAT mein load karne ke liye — typically production volume ka at least 20-30 percent. Is data ke against program run karo aur runtime aur memory consumption measure karo. Background jobs ke liye, verify karo ki yeh scheduled window mein complete hote hain. Key cheezein check karne ke liye: total runtime acceptable, koi time-out dumps nahi, memory usage system limits ke andar, database load doosre users ko impact nahi karta. Volume test pass karne wale programs production mein trust kiye ja sakte hain.
followupAnswerBank.interviewAnswer: Volume testing verifies that ABAP code performs acceptably with production-scale data, not just development test data. Approach: work with the basis team to load a representative sample of production data into QAT — typically at least 20-30 percent of production volume. Run the program against this data and measure runtime and memory consumption. For background jobs, verify they complete within the scheduled window. Key things to check: total runtime acceptable, no time-out dumps, memory usage within system limits, database load does not impact other users. Programs passing volume test can be trusted in production.
followupAnswerBank.realtimeExplanation: Go-live near-failure: custom MRP report ran 10 minutes in QAT with 1000 materials. Go-live had 80,000 materials — estimated 800 minutes. Business could not wait 13 hours for an overnight report. Emergency performance optimization was needed the week before go-live. Volume testing earlier would have identified this with time to fix properly.
followupAnswerBank.mistakes: Galti: 'QAT mein data kam hai, volume test nahi kar sakte.' Make the effort to load representative data. The cost of loading data once is far less than a production performance crisis during go-live.
followupAnswerBank.codeExample:
" Volume test: measure runtime
DATA: lv_start  TYPE timestampl,
      lv_end    TYPE timestampl,
      lv_elapsed TYPE p DECIMALS 3.
GET TIME STAMP FIELD lv_start.
" Run the actual program logic here with full data
SELECT matnr, mtart, meins FROM mara
  INTO TABLE @DATA(lt_mara) WHERE werks = '1000'.
GET TIME STAMP FIELD lv_end.
lv_elapsed = lv_end - lv_start.
WRITE: / |Runtime: { lv_elapsed } seconds|.

followupAnswerBank.question: What is authorization testing and why do developers often miss it?
followupAnswerBank.hinglishExplanation: Authorization testing matlab ABAP program ko ek test user ID ke saath run karna jiske paas sirf woh roles aur authorizations hain jo intended end users ke paas honge — developer ke admin-level access nahi. Developers ke pas typically SAP_ALL ya broad authorization profiles hote hain, isliye unka testing kabhi authorization failures trigger nahi karta. End users ke pas restricted roles hote hain. Common gaps found: custom code mein authority-check objects implement nahi kiye, programs company codes ke across data access karte hain jo user ko nahi dikhna chahiye, aur sensitive operations jaise posting ya deletion ke liye missing authorization checks. Transport se pehle hamesha business user equivalent ID ke saath test karo.
followupAnswerBank.interviewAnswer: Authorization testing means running the ABAP program with a test user ID that has only the roles and authorizations the intended end users will have — not the developer's admin-level access. Developers typically have SAP_ALL or broad authorization profiles, so their testing never triggers authorization failures. End users have restricted roles. Common gaps found: authority-check objects not implemented in custom code, programs accessing data across company codes the user should not see, and missing authorization checks for sensitive operations like posting or deletion. Always test with a business user equivalent ID before transport.
followupAnswerBank.realtimeExplanation: Common scenario: developer built a vendor payment report. Tested fine with own ID. Transported to production. Accounts Payable user tried to run — authorization error. The program had no AUTHORITY-CHECK for object F_LFA1_BUK (vendor by company code). Users could not run the report on go-live day. Fix needed emergency transport.
followupAnswerBank.mistakes: Galti: 'Authorization SAP BASIS log configure karenge, mujhe ABAP developer ko kya matlab.' Wrong — ABAP developer is responsible for implementing AUTHORITY-CHECK in custom code. BASIS assigns roles. Developer ensures the check is coded correctly.
followupAnswerBank.codeExample:
" Authorization check — developer must implement
AUTHORITY-CHECK OBJECT 'F_BKPF_BUK'
  ID 'BUKRS' FIELD lv_bukrs
  ID 'ACTVT' FIELD '03'.  " 03 = display
IF sy-subrc <> 0.
  MESSAGE 'No authorization for company code' TYPE 'E'.
  RETURN.  " Stop processing
ENDIF.

followupAnswerBank.question: How does peer code review improve code quality before production?
followupAnswerBank.hinglishExplanation: Peer code review ek structured process hai jahan transport request raise karne se pehle doosra ABAP developer code review karta hai. Reviewer business logic correctness, coding standards adherence, performance patterns, error handling completeness, security considerations, aur maintainability check karta hai. Author blind spots main value hain — code likhne ke baad, author easily nahi dekh sakta kya missing hai. Ek fresh reviewer immediately missing sy-subrc checks, SELECT star usage, ya logical gaps notice karta hai. Peer review tab most effective hota hai jab reviewers ek checklist use karte hain saare review sessions ke across consistent coverage ensure karne ke liye.
followupAnswerBank.interviewAnswer: Peer code review is a structured process where another ABAP developer reviews the code before the transport request is raised. The reviewer checks business logic correctness, adherence to coding standards, performance patterns, error handling completeness, security considerations, and maintainability. Author blind spots are the main value — after writing code, the author cannot easily see what is missing. A fresh reviewer immediately notices missing sy-subrc checks, SELECT star usage, or logical gaps. Peer review is most effective when reviewers use a checklist to ensure consistent coverage across all review sessions.
followupAnswerBank.realtimeExplanation: Code review caught: developer wrote a goods receipt program that correctly processed materials but did not handle the case where vendor was blocked for payment. Reviewer asked: what happens if the vendor is payment-blocked? No check existed. Without review, the program would have posted GR for blocked vendors — a compliance violation.
followupAnswerBank.mistakes: Galti: 'Mera code bahut simple hai, review ki zaroorat nahi.' Simple code bhi review benefits from. And often what seems simple to the author has assumptions the reviewer catches. No code should skip review.
followupAnswerBank.codeExample:
" Code review checklist items (review these):
" [ ] SELECT * removed, specific columns named
" [ ] sy-subrc checked after every SELECT/READ
" [ ] FOR ALL ENTRIES: empty check before usage
" [ ] Hardcoded values removed (company code, plant)
" [ ] Exception handling: TRY-CATCH implemented
" [ ] AUTHORITY-CHECK for sensitive operations
" [ ] ATC: zero priority-1 findings confirmed

followupAnswerBank.question: What is UAT and what is the ABAP developer's role in it?
followupAnswerBank.hinglishExplanation: UAT User Acceptance Testing hai jahan business users validate karte hain ki ABAP program ek realistic test environment mein unke actual business requirements meet karta hai. UAT mein ABAP developer ka role: representative test data ke saath QAT prepare karo, testing ke dauran issues ko jaldi fix karne ke liye available raho, root cause aur fix ke saath saare bugs found document karo, har fix ke baad regression testing ensure karo, aur production transport raise karne se pehle business user ya functional consultant se formal sign-off lo. UAT business logic gaps catch karta hai jo technical testing miss karta hai — incorrect field labels, wrong number formats, missing edge cases jo business daily encounter karta hai.
followupAnswerBank.interviewAnswer: UAT is User Acceptance Testing where business users validate that the ABAP program meets their actual business requirements in a realistic test environment. The ABAP developer's role in UAT: prepare QAT with representative test data, be available during testing to fix issues quickly, document all bugs found with root cause and fix, ensure regression testing after each fix, and obtain formal sign-off from the business user or functional consultant before raising the production transport. UAT catches business logic gaps that technical testing misses — incorrect field labels, wrong number formats, missing edge cases the business encounters daily.
followupAnswerBank.realtimeExplanation: UAT finding: Developer built a sales register report. Output showed correct amounts technically. Business user during UAT: amounts showing in USD but our reporting is in INR — can you add currency conversion? This business requirement was never discussed in technical spec. UAT caught it before production. Without UAT, report would have been rejected in production causing delays.
followupAnswerBank.mistakes: Galti: 'Functional consultant ne sign kiya toh enough hai, business user UAT ki zaroorat nahi.' Functional consultant is not the end user. Business users discover practical issues that functional consultants miss. Both sign-offs are needed.
followupAnswerBank.codeExample:
" UAT preparation: Provide test scenario documentation
" Scenario 1: Normal vendor invoice posting
"   Input: Vendor 100001, Amount 50000 INR, Plant 1000
"   Expected: Document number generated, GL posted

" Scenario 2: Vendor blocked for payment
"   Input: Blocked vendor 100005
"   Expected: Error message shown, no posting

" Scenario 3: Duplicate invoice check
"   Input: Same invoice number for same vendor
"   Expected: Warning/Error based on config

followupAnswerBank.question: What is the transport sequence and why must it be followed?
followupAnswerBank.hinglishExplanation: Standard SAP transport sequence DEV se QAT se PRD hai — Development client se Quality Assurance Testing client se Production client. Yeh sequence mandatory hai kyunki yeh production se pehle ek controlled testing gate provide karta hai. DEV development aur unit testing ke liye hai. QAT mein functional testing, volume testing, UAT, aur integration testing ke liye representative data hai. PRD real business data ke saath live hai. QAT bypass karna — urgent fixes ke liye bhi — untested code production tak pahunchne ka risk hai. Emergency hotfixes ke liye, ek fast-tracked lekin abbreviated testing process abhi bhi follow karna chahiye, complete bypass nahi.
followupAnswerBank.interviewAnswer: The standard SAP transport sequence is DEV to QAT to PRD — Development client to Quality Assurance Testing client to Production client. This sequence is mandatory because it provides a controlled testing gate before production. DEV is for development and unit testing. QAT has representative data for functional testing, volume testing, UAT, and integration testing. PRD is live with real business data. Bypassing QAT — even for urgent fixes — risks untested code reaching production. For emergency hotfixes, a fast-tracked but abbreviated testing process should still be followed, not a complete bypass.
followupAnswerBank.realtimeExplanation: Emergency situation: production issue on Friday evening. Manager pressured direct PRD transport from DEV to fix quickly. Team insisted on at least a 30-minute QAT smoke test. During smoke test: the fix was correct but introduced a new error in an adjacent function. Caught in QAT. Proper fix made. PRD transport done correctly. Had we bypassed QAT, new bug would have gone to production along with the fix.
followupAnswerBank.mistakes: Galti: 'Small change hai — directly PRD transport kar do.' No change is too small to skip testing. Even a one-line change can have unexpected side effects. The transport sequence exists precisely for these situations.
followupAnswerBank.codeExample:
" Transport sequence discipline:
" Step 1: DEV — develop and unit test
" Step 2: Release task in SE09/SE10
" Step 3: QAT transport import — functional test
" Step 4: ATC check on QAT if not done in DEV
" Step 5: UAT sign-off from business user
" Step 6: Change Management approval (production)
" Step 7: PRD transport import in maintenance window
" Step 8: Post-go-live smoke test in production

followupAnswerBank.question: What do you do if a bug is found in production after transport?
followupAnswerBank.hinglishExplanation: Jab transport ke baad production bug found hota hai: pehle impact assess karo — kitne users affected, data corruption hai ya nahi, yeh business-critical hai ya cosmetic. Agar critical hai, transport rollback vs hotfix evaluate karo — rollback faster hai lekin same transport mein dusre valid changes revert kar sakta hai. Hotfix ke liye: DEV mein fix create karo, ATC run karo, QAT mein specific scenario ke saath abbreviated lekin mandatory testing perform karo jo fail hua, expedited approval lo, aur production tak transport karo. Stabilization ke baad: identify karne ke liye root cause analysis conduct karo ki kaunsa testing step missed tha. Finding document karo aur repeat prevent karne ke liye testing checklist update karo.
followupAnswerBank.interviewAnswer: When a production bug is found after transport: first assess impact — how many users affected, is there data corruption, is it business-critical or cosmetic. If critical, evaluate rollback of the transport versus a hotfix — rollback is faster but may revert other valid changes in the same transport. For hotfix: create the fix in DEV, run ATC, perform abbreviated but mandatory testing in QAT with the specific scenario that failed, get expedited approval, and transport to production. After stabilization: conduct root cause analysis to identify what testing step was missed. Document the finding and update the testing checklist to prevent repeat.
followupAnswerBank.realtimeExplanation: Production bug post-go-live: material valuation report showing zero for new materials. Root cause: new material type not handled in custom code — worked for old types only. Testing was done only with existing materials. Hotfix in 2 hours, QAT test in 30 minutes, PRD in evening maintenance window. Lesson added to checklist: always test with newly created master data, not just existing.
followupAnswerBank.mistakes: Galti: Production fix seedha DEV se PRD — because emergency. This creates another potential bug. Even emergencies require abbreviated QAT smoke test — 30 minutes minimum. No exception.
followupAnswerBank.codeExample:
" Post-production bug handling pattern:
" 1. Assess: how many affected?
SELECT COUNT(*) FROM ztransaction_log
  INTO @DATA(lv_affected)
  WHERE error_flag = 'X'
    AND process_date = @sy-datum.
" 2. Fix DEV → ATC → QAT smoke test → PRD
" 3. Root cause: which test case was missed?
" 4. Update test checklist with new scenario
